title: St. Pachomius — Father of Koinonia — St. Petronilla & St. Isidore of Chios
narrative:
St. Pachomius the Great, Father of Cenobitic Monasticism, St. Petronilla the Daughter of the Apostle Peter, and St. Isidore of Chios the Sailor-Martyr

On the fourteenth of Ginbot, the holy Church celebrates the feast of the holy, God-bearing, and venerable Pachomius the Great, the Egyptian soldier turned monk who became the father of the cenobitic — the common — life, the man who translated the solitary wisdom of the desert fathers into the institutional form of the monastery and thereby made the fruits of the monastic life available to the multitude who could not sustain the absolute solitude of the hermit. Pachomius founded his first community on the island of Tabennisi in the Thebaid around the year three hundred and twenty, and by the time of his death in three hundred and forty-eight he had established nine monasteries for men and two for women, containing in total several thousand monks and nuns. His Rule — preserved in Greek, Latin, Coptic, and Ethiopic translations — is one of the foundational documents of Christian monastic life, the ancestor of every Rule that followed it, the first sustained attempt to translate the Gospel counsel of total self-giving into a communal institutional form that could be lived by many rather than only by the exceptional few.

The theology of Pachomius, in the Ethiopian tradition, is inseparable from the theology of koinonia — the Greek word for community, fellowship, participation — that his Rule embodies. The monastic community Pachomius envisioned was not a collection of solitaries who happened to share a building but a body, an organism, a living icon of the divine Trinity in which the distinct persons maintain their distinctness while subsisting in a unity of love and purpose. His Rule structures every detail of the common life — the hours of prayer, the distribution of labour, the governance of the community, the formation of new members — around this Trinitarian model, so that the monastery becomes a school not only of personal virtue but of communal life as a participation in divine life. In a country whose earliest monastic tradition came directly from Egypt and owes its foundation partly to Pachomian inspiration, this feast is a celebration of the Ethiopian church's own origins.

Saint Petronilla is one of the most beloved of the Roman martyrs and one of the most intriguing: the tradition identifies her as the daughter of the Apostle Peter, though the precise nature of this relationship — biological daughter, spiritual daughter, woman healed and adopted into Peter's household — has never been definitively resolved, and the tradition is content to leave the question open. What the tradition preserves with consistency is that she was a woman of particular physical beauty who attracted the unwanted attention of a Roman noble named Flaccus, who demanded her hand in marriage; that she asked for and received three days to consider the matter; that she spent those three days in continuous prayer and fasting; and that at the end of them she received the Eucharist, lay down, and died. Her death — peaceful, voluntary, neither by torture nor by execution but by a holy dying that followed the reception of the Body and Blood — is the tradition's most concentrated image of the Eucharist as the direct cause of the holy death: she received the food of eternity and was drawn immediately into it.

Saint Isidore of Chios is the great discovery of this day: a sailor in the Roman fleet who was martyred on the island of Chios in the third century, and whose story has a maritime texture that distinguishes it from the predominantly agrarian or urban settings of most martyrologies. Isidore was identified as a Christian by his refusal to offer sacrifice at a port festival, arrested by his ship's commander, and executed with a brutality that the tradition attributes in part to the commander's personal outrage at the calm with which Isidore received each new torture. The detail the tradition preserves with greatest emphasis is that Isidore, throughout his ordeal, kept his eyes on the sea — the same sea that had carried him across the Mediterranean in his years of naval service — as if the horizon were a theological statement, a visible image of the God who cannot be encompassed. The sailor who found God in the open water, alongside Pachomius who found God in the ordered community and Petronilla who found God in the Eucharist, completes a day whose meditation is on the inexhaustible variety of the divine meeting-places: community, sacrament, and the infinite horizon are equally the places where the living God is encountered. Amen.
